text.title: Stakeholder Engagement Framework
text.summary: The five engagement levels, assessment matrix, level-transition strategies, and warning signs that signal engagement degradation.

section.title: Engagement Levels
section.p: Each stakeholder is assessed at a point in time against five levels:

list.item: Unaware (U) — stakeholder does not know the project exists or does not understand its relevance.
Resistant (R) — aware but actively or passively opposed; may block decisions or withhold resources.
Neutral (N) — aware but indifferent; neither advances nor hinders the project.
Supportive (S) — aware and positively engaged; provides inputs, advocates informally.
Leading (L) — actively champions the project; removes barriers, drives adoption, takes public ownership.

rule.statement: Every engagement level assessment must cite at least one behavioral signal; "seems supportive" without evidence defaults to "unknown", not Supportive.
rule.rationale: Label-based assessments without behavioral grounding create false confidence; a PM who believes a stakeholder is Supportive based on polite email responses misses the resistance that surfaces at the critical decision gate.

section.title: Level-Transition Strategies
section.p: Strategies differ by the transition being targeted:

list.item: Unaware to Neutral — inform; tailor message to their specific interests; use trusted intermediaries.
Resistant to Neutral — listen first, document concerns in the stakeholder's own words; involve in problem-solving; give veto power on specific concerns to build trust; find common ground.
Neutral to Supportive — demonstrate benefits relevant to their personal goals; ask for their input to generate ownership; show quick wins early.
Supportive to Leading — assign visible roles (launch spokesperson, steering committee seat); recognize contributions publicly; share strategic information; empower specific decisions.

rule.statement: For Resistant stakeholders, document their concerns verbatim before designing the strategy; strategies built on assumed concerns miss the real objection and waste engagement effort.
rule.rationale: PM assumptions about why someone is resistant are often wrong; the actual concern (a personal risk, a process implication, an unmet need) requires direct conversation to surface, and addressing the stated concern produces visible trust.

section.title: Warning Signs
section.p: Observable signals that indicate engagement is degrading — requires immediate outreach:

list.item: Declining meeting attendance — sends delegate or stops attending entirely.
Response latency increases — emails taking days to answer that previously received same-day replies.
Negative comments in public forums — criticism raised in steering meetings rather than 1:1.
Going around the project team — raising concerns directly with the executive sponsor.
Silence in meetings — previously vocal stakeholder becomes passive.

rule.statement: Maintain a 30-day rolling engagement log per high-power stakeholder; level changes must be confirmed by two independent signals, not a single interaction.
rule.rationale: Single-signal level changes based on one meeting or one email are unreliable; cross-cultural communication differences, temporary workload spikes, and politeness norms all produce false signals.
